coredata.dc:title: A chemiluminescence method induced by microplasma jet for nitrites detection and the miniature detection system using smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Yi
Zhou, Han
Li, Wei
Yao, Cheng
Wang, Jianhua
Zhao, Yong
coredata.dc:description: A method of luminol-diazonium chemiluminescence (CL) induced by microplasma for hazardous substance detection is proposed. The luminol-diazonium CL is caused by microplasma jet, rather than hydrogen peroxide reagent or other oxidizing agents. The CL intensity is increased by the concentration of nitrites. Based on the process of microplasma generation and CL mechanism, the optimal work conditions of the method are obtained. The linear range for nitrites detection is 0.03–1 mmol L−1 with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.01 mmol L−1. Furthermore, a miniature system using test paper and smartphone is designed for nitrites detection in emergency. The detection system is confined in the custom-tailored shell which is only 28 cm in length, 18 cm in width and 10 cm in height. After microplasma jet treatment, the color of the test paper changes with the NO2 − concentration. The photographs of the test paper are taken by the built-in camera of smartphone and analyzed by visiting the website via smartphone. The LOD is 1 mmol L−1 obtained by the CL miniature detection system based on test paper and smartphone. The accuracy, reliability and practicability of the proposed method is verified in this paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microplasma jet
Luminol-diazonium
Chemiluminescence
NO2 − Detection
Miniature detection system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The experimental set-up of spectrometer-based CL method
The experimental set-up of smartphone-based CL system
The CL method for NO2 − detection
Miniature CL system for NO2 − detection using test paper and smartphone
Interference test and the detection of real-world samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the smartphone-based CL system induced by microplasma jet.
The process of color acquisition and analysis by the smartphone-based CL system.
CL spectra of luminol-diazonium, luminol-diazonium induced by H2O2 and luminol-diazonium induced by microplasma jet, respectively. Details of experimental parameters are given in Table S1.

Dependences of △ICL and S/B on (a) discharging voltage, (b) distance between the two electrodes and (c) argon flow rate, when 0.5 mmol L−1 NO2 − is spiked into the luminol-diazonium solution. Details of experimental parameters are given in Table S1.

Dependence of △ICL and S/B on the concentration of (a) luminol, (b) HNO3, (c) PPD, (d) NaOH, respectively, when 0.5 mmol L−1 NO2 − is spiked into the luminol-diazonium solution. Details of experimental parameters are given in Table S1.
The calibration curve for NO2 − detection obtained by the proposed CL method Details of experimental parameters are given in Table S1.

Dependence of B/R value on the distance between the outlet of microplasma and the test paper; the sample solution with 10 mmol L−1 NO2 − is dropped on the test paper. Details of other experimental parameters are given in Table S1.
The calibration curve for NO2 − detection obtained by the CL miniature system using smartphone and test paper. Details of experimental parameters are given in Table S1.
The detection results of NO2 − in real-word samples obtained by spectrometer-based and smartphone-based and certified methods (n = 3, P = 0.95).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The spoiled vegetable juice was 10-fold diluted before determination using spectrometer-based method and certified method.

head.ce:title: A chemiluminescence method induced by microplasma jet for nitrites detection and the miniature detection system using smartphone

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Precision Optical Sensing and Measurement Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method of luminol-diazonium chemiluminescence (CL) induced by microplasma for hazardous substance detection is proposed. The luminol-diazonium CL is caused by microplasma jet, rather than hydrogen peroxide reagent or other oxidizing agents. The CL intensity is increased by the concentration of nitrites. Based on the process of microplasma generation and CL mechanism, the optimal work conditions of the method are obtained. The linear range for nitrites detection is 0.03–1 mmol L−1 with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.01 mmol L−1. Furthermore, a miniature system using test paper and smartphone is designed for nitrites detection in emergency. The detection system is confined in the custom-tailored shell which is only 28 cm in length, 18 cm in width and 10 cm in height. After microplasma jet treatment, the color of the test paper changes with the NO2 − concentration. The photographs of the test paper are taken by the built-in camera of smartphone and analyzed by visiting the website via smartphone. The LOD is 1 mmol L−1 obtained by the CL miniature detection system based on test paper and smartphone. The accuracy, reliability and practicability of the proposed method is verified in this paper.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrites are one kind of widespread nitrogen-containing compounds in nature [1]. Although the appearance and taste of the sodium nitrite are similar to salt, sodium nitrite is toxic and may induce food poisoning [2]. The nitrites can oxidize the methemoglobins of the body and lead to the terrible effect on oxygen transport [3]. It is turned out that only 0.2 g–0.5 g intake of nitrite causes poisoning, and 3 g intake may result in death for human beings [4]. As the standard set by the World Health Organization (WHO), the limit of nitrite in drinking water is 1.0 mg L−1 [5]. Therefore, it is significant to develop the sensitive and effective method for nitrites detection.

At present, the nitrites can be detected by the methods of capillary electrophoresis, voltammetry, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, chemiluminescence and so on [6–8]. Among them, the chemiluminescence (CL)-based methods are sensitive in wide linear range and have a rapid response [9]. The CL methods for nitrites detection are widely used in environmental analysis and food safety [10,11]. Linlin Liu et al. reported a flow injection CL assay for the determination of trace nitrite [12]. The nitrite quenched the CL of CdTe quantum dots (QDs)–H2O2–trace hemoglobin (Hb) system. A computerized ultra-weak luminescence analyzer was employed as the detector and the nitrites could be determined in the range of 1.0 × 10−9-8.0 × 10−7 mol L−1 with the limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 × 10−10 mol L−1. This method was proved to apply for nitrite detection in water samples successfully. The luminol-diazonium-H2O2 system is reported [13]. Part of the luminol-diazonium in alkaline solution gets rid of the nitrogen to form hydroxyl luminol. The hydroxyl luminol and the remaining luminol-diazonium are then activated to the corresponding diformylhydrazine radicals by •OH radicals produced from hydrogen peroxide. The two diformylhydrazine radicals are oxidized into peroxide intermediates, and decompose into the excited N2 +-phthalic anion and hydroxyphthalic anion due to the unstable of the peroxide intermediates, which finally produce CL [14]. It is found that the addition of p-phenylenediamine (PPD) could improve the yield of luminol-diazonium [15].

Microplasma is produced by confining the dimensions of plasmas to 1 mm or less [16]. It is applied in the fields of spectroscopy, surface treatment, biomedicine recently due to the advantages of low power consumption, small in size and high energy density [17,18]. The high-energy electrons, ions, and radicals produced from microplasma play a significant role in chemical reactions. For example, the reactive species produced by microplasma in various gas mixtures inhibit the antibacterial effects [19], and the technique based on dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) for various reactive oxygen species generation is used to remove antibiotics in waste activated sludge [20]. Moreover, Naoki Shirai et al. observed that the microplasma radiation caused chemiluminescence in luminol solution [21]. It is shown that the radicals of •OH and H2O2 in microplasma can convert to O2 − radicals by the route,
(1) •OH + H2O2→O2 − + H3O+

The O2 − radicals can be used to induce luminol CL instead of the reagent of hydrogen peroxide, which is corrosive and should be stored in shade. The CL system induced by nonthermal microplama is convenient and effective, which is more suitable for the detection in field or in emergency. The discharging section is inexpensive. In addition, it is proved that the short contact between human finger and microplasma jet is safe [22]. In our previous work, a microplasma-based CL system is developed for 3-nitropropionic acid detection [23].

In this paper, the microplasma jet is employed for luminol-diazonium oxidization to produce CL. It is found that the CL of the luminol-diazonium can be enhanced with the existence of NO2 −. The wavelength of the peak in the CL system is 490 nm. With 3.0 mmol L−1 luminol, 60 s analysis time and 1.0 mL solution in the sample cell, the detection limit of NO2 − is 0.01 mmol L−1 obtained by the method, which is below the upper limit of drinking water set by WHO. The above detection method can be used to develop an available and miniature system. Rather than the sample cell, the test paper is adopted as sample carrier. The smartphone in pocket is used as detector. The built-in camera of smartphone is employed to catch the CL variation. The weight of the miniature system is 1.9 kg. Although the sensitivity is inferior to the proposed spectrometer-based system, the portable detection system can be used to estimate the pollution level initially. In this way, NO2 − can be rapidly detected in emergency without spectrometer. The practicality and reliability of the detection method and miniature system are verified by real-world samples determination.

The concentrated nitric acid, luminol, p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and NaNO2 reagents were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (L. A., USA). The reagents of NaOH and H2O2 (30%) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). High-purity Ar (99.99%) was used to maintain the microplasma jet. DI water (≥18 MΩ cm) was used through the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The experimental set-up of spectrometer-based CL method
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental set-up for NO2 − detection using spectrometer as detector is illustrated in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1, the set-up is composed of discharging section, excitation light source and detector. The set-up is established based on our previous work [23]. The detailed description is stated in the Supplementary Materials. The argon stream flows through the designed microplasma generator. With the high alternating voltage on the electrodes, the microplasma jet is generated. A 1.2 mL PTFE sample cell is located below the microplasma, thus the microplasma jet reaches the center of liquid level. In the sample cell, 3.0 mmol L−1 luminol, 0.24 mol L−1 HNO3, 2.5 mmol L−1 PPD, 0.5 mol L−1 NaOH are added into the solution with different concentrations of NO2 − in sequence. The active radicals in microplasma jet contact with the solution in the sample cell and produce strong chemiluminescence under 375 nm UV light. The optical signal is transmitted through the multimode fiber and recorded by the spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The experimental set-up of smartphone-based CL system
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above detection method using spectrometer, the smartphone-based chemiluminescence experimental set-up for NO2 − detection is developed and shown in Fig. 1 . The detection system includes a microplasma generator, a shelving unit for the test paper, a flashlight which emits 375 nm UV light and a smartphone. The UV light flashlight is employed to enhance the CL intensity when the detection is needed in field or in emergency. The microplasma generator is described in the section of “2.2 The experimental set-up of spectrometer-based CL method”. The test paper is prepared from the purchasable bamboo pulp paper (Sichuan Blue Ticket Commodity Co., Ltd., China) and horizontally laid on the slide, which acts as the movable part of the shelving unit. The size of the test paper, the slide and the PTFE layer under the slide are marked in Fig. 1, respectively.

The detection process is illustrated in Fig. 2 . Firstly, the well-cut paper is placed on glass slide (76 mm × 25 mm) and soaked by 1 mL NaOH solution (0.5 mol L−1). The NaOH solution immerses the pulp paper absolutely, and no aqueous solution overflows from the glass slide. The paper with NaOH solution is dried at 60 °C in a heating oven. For sample introduction, 1 mL prepared sample solution (NO2 − solution with 3.0 mmol L−1 luminol, 0.24 mol L−1 HNO3 and 2.5 mmol L−1 PPD) is dropped on the prepared paper by pipettor.

Secondly, the paper is treated by microplasma jet immediately. After 60 s treatment, the test paper on slide is moved to the right end of the PTFE layer for UV light irradiation and photo taken. The photograph is taken by the built-in camera of the smartphone (Huawei Nova 6, 1/50 s exposure time). A custom-tailored shell (28 cm in length × 18 cm in width × 10 cm in height) is set outside the detection system for structural support. The smartphone is fixed on the shell and the distance between the smartphone and the test paper is 16 cm. A 45° angle between smartphone and the shell is adjusted. Finally, the photo is uploaded to the website of “color hunter” (www.colorhunter.com), and the B and R data (RGB color model) of CL area can be quickly obtained. The B/R values of the test paper are used for NO2 − quantitative analysis.

The process of luminol-diazonium formation is illustrated as reaction (2) to (4). The presence of PPD-diazonium accelerates the conversion of luminol to luminol-diazonium, increasing the overall yield of luminol-diazonium. The diazotizations of PPD and luminol both occur in the acid environment [15].
(2)
(3)
(4) Lu + N2 +-PPD → N2 +-Lu + PPD

For the CL of luminol-diazonium, alkaline solution is needed [24]. When luminol, HNO3, PPD, NO2 − and NaOH are added in the solution in sequence, the peak at 490 nm appears with 375 nm irradiation, as shown in Fig. 3 . In the experiment, the concentrations of luminol, HNO3, PPD, NO2 − and NaOH are 3.0 mmol L−1, 0.24 mol L−1, 2.5 mmol L−1, 0.5 mmol L−1 and 0.5 mol L−1, respectively. The black curve of Fig. 3 is the spectrum without oxidant under 375 nm irradiation. When 1.2 mol L−1 H2O2 is added for oxidation, the intensity is enhanced obviously under UV light irradiation, which is shown as the blue curve. The oxidant is necessary to enhance the CL through the following reaction,
(5) N2 +-Lu + O2− →N2 +-phthalic anion + hγ
In fact, the O2 − can be also obtained from microplasma (reaction 1). As shown as the red curve in Fig. 3, it is observed that the CL intensity can be also enhanced with 60 s treatment of microplasma jet. It is observed that CL is first generated on the interface of the liquid, and then CL diffuses throughout the solution.

Microplasma jet provides radicals for luminol-diazonium CL [25]. The radicals of H2O2 and ·OH can turn into O2 − radicals for oxidation [21]. With microplasma jet treatment, the CL enhanced gradually. The CL spectra obtained after 10 s–60 s treatment are shown in Fig. S2. For microplasma generation, the discharging voltage and the distance between two electrodes are the important factors. When the discharging voltage is higher than 2.87 kV, the argon stream between two electrodes breakdown, and the microplasma is generated. With the higher discharging voltage in the range of 2.87–4.36 kV, the stable microplasma can be formed. We use the net CL signal △ICL (△ICL = intensified CL - blank CL) to evaluate the variation of CL intensity, and use the ratio of △ICL to the blank (S/B) caused by 0.5 mmol L−1 NO2 − to estimate the signal to noise (SNR). It is shown in Fig. 4 (a) that △ICL and S/B are significantly enhanced with the discharging voltage. This is because that with the higher voltage field, the number of high-energy electrons increases in the microplasma, thus causes more active plasma chemistry [26]. However, when the discharging voltage reaches 4.66 kV, the discharge becomes unstable. Therefore, 4.36 kV discharging voltage is appropriate for microplasma generation of the method. A miniature regulator is employed to control the neon power supply here, and 4.36 kV is equal to the 7/10 gear of the regulator. With this discharging voltage, the power consumption of the microplamsa is as low as 0.07 W.

The dependences of △ICL and value of S/B on distance between two electrodes are also investigated. The results are shown in Fig. 4 (b). The maximum △ICL and value of S/B are obtained when the distance is 15 mm. This may because the energy density decreases with the distance between the two electrodes [27]. However, the air around the electrodes is breakdown when the distance between two electrodes is less than 15 mm, which causes unstable discharge.

The argon stream is used to maintain the microplasma. On the other hand, the flow rate of the argon stream determines the extension of the microplasma jet from the outlet. The inset in Fig. 4 (c) shows that with higher argon flow rate, the extension microplasma jet becomes longer and stronger. The liquid interface is set 5 mm below the outlet of microplasma jet. The contact area between the solution surface and microplasma jet is enlarged along with the argon flow rate. The influence of argon flow rate on △ICL and the value of S/B illustrates that higher flow rate of argon stream brings better detection result. Considering the argon consumption and the maximum flow rate controlled by the flowmeter, 1000 mL min−1 argon flow rate is adopted for the further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CL method for NO2 − detection
ce:section.ce:para: The choice of luminol concentration should be sufficient for CL. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), △ICL increases with the luminol concentration in the range of 1–7 mmol L−1. However, when 3.0 mmol L−1 luminol is selected, the value of S/B is maximum, showing a great signal to noise.

Based on the mechanism of luminol-diazonium formation (described in “3.1 Microplasma jet-induced chemiluminescence”), the acidity and the PPD concentration of the aqueous solution in the sample cell are important to chemiluminescence and NO2 − detection. It is illustrated in Fig. 5 (b) that △ICL and the value of S/B both increase with the concentration of HNO3 in the range of 0.20 mol L−1 to 0.24 mol L−1. The results prove that the diazotization of luminol takes place in the acid solution. When 0.24 mol L−1 HNO3 are added to the solution, the maximum △ICL and S/B value induced by 0.5 mmol L−1 NO2 − is obtained. The reagent of PPD, acts as catalyzer, can accelerate the CL reaction obviously. When different concentration of PPD addition, the results are displayed in Fig. 5 (c). With 2.5 mmol L−1 PPD addition, the maximum S/B induced by 0.5 mmol L−1 NO2 − can be acquired. For the oxidation of luminol-diazonium, NaOH should be added in the aqueous solution after diazotization. The effects of NaOH concentration on △ICL and S/B are displayed in Fig. 5 (d). For the satisfied SNR, the concentration of NaOH is adopted as 0.5 mol L−1.

The microplasma jet reaches the liquid in sample cell vertically. The distance between the end of the outlet and the interface of the aqueous solution is the actual length of the microplasma jet. Fig. S3 shows the dependences of △ICL and S/B value on the length of the microplasma jet. It is found that liquid surface should be close to the outlet of the microplasma jet. The electron density of the microplasma jet decreases with the extension length of the microplasma jet [28]. However, when the distance is less than 5 mm, the liquid in the sample cell may be splashed out of the cell by the argon stream.

According to the CL mechanism induced by microplasma for NO2 − detection, the optimal experimental condition is investigated and summarized in Table S1. With 1.0 mL sample introduction, the proposed method can accomplish NO2 − detection in the concentration range from 0.03 to 1 mmol L−1, showing a linear relationship with △ICL. The calibration curve for NO2 − detection is shown in Fig. 6 . The detection limit (LOD) of the method is 0.01 mmol L−1. Moreover, the dependence of CL intensity on different time after microplasma jet treatment is investigated and shown in Fig. S4. It is illustrated that the CL intensity remains constant in 4 min. Therefore, the results should be collected in the limited time for reliable measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Miniature CL system for NO2 − detection using test paper and smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above detection method, a miniature system is further developed using test paper and smartphone. The diagram of the miniature system is shown in Fig. 1 and the operation process is stated in “2.3 The experimental set-up of smartphone-based CL system” in detail. The total weight of the miniature detection system is only 1.9 kg.

The test paper is employed as sample carrier instead of sample cell in the miniature detection system. In the experiment, the bamboo pulp paper is used for test paper preparation because there is barely fluorescence emission with 375 nm UV light irradiation. The photos of bare Whatman paper and bamboo pulp paper under irradiation are shown in Fig. S5. Besides, the bamboo pulp paper is also available and low-cost.

Due to the flammable of the test paper, the temperature of the microplasma jet should be taken into consideration. As measured by thermometer, the temperature of the microplasma jet is only 43 °C, which is far from the kindling point of the test paper. When the distance between outlet of the microplasma jet and test paper is set in the range of 5–24 mm, the paper will not be damaged by the microplasma jet. The photos of the test paper after miciroplasma treatment are shown in the inset (a) of Fig. 7 . It is illustrated that the high-energy electrons from microplasma jet cannot ignite test paper. The inset (b) shows that the CL presents on the test paper after microplasma treatment under UV light irradiation. The photos are recorded by the build-in camera of the smartphone and uploaded to the website of “color hunter” (www.colorhunter.com). The values of red-green-blue (RGB) are analyzed. As shown in Fig. 7, the value of B/R decreases with the distance between of microplasma jet.

The feasibility of the developed miniature system using smartphone and test paper is studied. The area of test paper with the microplasma jet treatment shows CL emission under light irradiation. With the increase of NO2 − concentration, the colors acquired by camera are changed, which are shown in the inset of Fig. 8 . The B and R values are also marked beside the photos. The concentration of NO2 − can be quantitatively analyzed based on the ratio of B to R. The linear range of the miniature detection system is 2–50 mmol L−1, with the LOD of 1 mmol L−1. The calibration curve can be approximately expressed as B/R = 0.044c (NO2 −) +1.446. Although the sensitivity is inferior to the method based on spectrometer (described in “3.3 The CL method for NO
2
−
detection”), the system using smartphone could meet the requirement for rapid NO2 − detection in emergency. Moreover, the same sample (10 mmol L−1 NO2 −) is measured for 7 times, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the B/R value is 1.0%, demonstrated the repeatability of the miniature detection system. The miniature detection system is cost-effective, small in size and easy to operate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference test and the detection of real-world samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the interferences of coexisting compounds in the solution, a series foreign compounds including sucrose, glucose, amino acids and metal ions were added to the NO2 − solution, respectively. The results show that 2000-fold K+, Na+, 150-fold Mg2+, glucose, sucrose, glutamate and glycine can be tolerated within the error of ±5% when the foreign compounds spiked in 0.1 mmol L−1 NO2 − solution, respectively.

The proposed method using spectrometer and the developed smartphone-based system were applied to detect a series of real-world samples, including environmental water (tap water, rainwater and river water collected from Qinhuangdao, China) and spoiled vegetable juice. The environmental water samples were filtered by 20 μm filter membrane before detection. The results are shown in Table 1 . It is found that there is no NO2 − in tap water, and the concentrations of NO2 − in rainwater, river water and the spoiled vegetable juice detected by spectrometer-based method are 0.05 mmol L−1, 0.07 mmol L−1 and 8.3 mmol L−1, respectively. The NO2 − concentration of spoiled vegetable juice detected by the system using smartphone is 8 mmol L−1. When 0.20 mmol L−1 and 7 mmol L−1 NO2 − were spiked in the samples, the recoveries of tap water, rainwater and river water were all in the range of 95%–105%, respectively. The results detected by the proposed spectrometer-based CL method and the miniature smartphone-based system are consistent with the results obtained by UV–Vis spectrophotometry, showing that the proposed method and the miniature system are both reliable for the analysis of NO2 −.

A method of luminol-diazonium chemiluminescence induced by microplasma for nitrites detection without the reagent of hydrogen peroxide was developed. The nonthermal microplasma plays a significant role in chemiluminescence. Based on the mechanism of CL induced by microplasma, the optimal experimental conditions were investigated. The proposed method is cost-effective, small in size and easy to operate. The reliability and practicability of the detection method and the miniature system were verified by actual samples determination. Further, a miniature CL system using test paper as sample carrier and smartphone as detector was developed for nitrites detection in emergency. Although the sensitivity is inferior to the spectrometer-based method, the miniature detection system can be used to estimate the pollution level initially, making it easy to take corresponding measures for pollution abatement. The test paper-based system is benefit from the development of photoelectric detection technology. The smaller, faster and more accurate detection methods for hazardous substances may appear with the prosperous of electronic information technology.

Yi Cai: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Han Zhou: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Validation. Wei Li: Methodology. Cheng Yao: Investigation, Validation. Jianhua Wang: Methodology. Yong Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21904017), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (F2020501040)，the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2223035) and Scientiﬁc and Technical Research Project in Colleges and Universities of Hebei Province (BJK2022069).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.341339.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of nitrite and nitrate
Food Chem. Toxicol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sepahvand, F. Ghasemi, H.M. Seyed Hosseini, Plasmonic nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of nitrite and nitrate, Food Chem. Toxicol. 149 (2021) 112025.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin Ni-MOF nanobelts-derived composite for high sensitive detection of nitrite
Front. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Meng, X. Xiao, H. Pang, Ultrathin Ni-MOF nanobelts-derived composite for high sensitive detection of nitrite, Front. Chem. 8 (2020) 330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Peroxynitrous-acid-induced chemiluminescence detection of nitrite based on microfluidic chip
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wu, X. Wang, Y. Lin, Y. Zheng, J.M. Lin, Peroxynitrous-acid-induced chemiluminescence detection of nitrite based on microfluidic chip, Talanta 154 (2016) 73-79.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of Bi-directional detection for ascorbic acid and sodium nitrite based on Eu-containing luminescent polyoxometalate
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Fu, W. Gao, T. Yu, L. Bi, Study of Bi-directional detection for ascorbic acid and sodium nitrite based on Eu-containing luminescent polyoxometalate, Talanta 195 (2019) 463-471.
